item.title: Country blues
item.description: Blues | American folk music → Country blues Period: c. 1920s–1940s (recorded era); roots c.

item.title: Jump blues
item.description: Blues → Jump blues Period: Late 1930s–mid-1950s Location: Kansas City, Los Angeles, New York, Chicago Key influences: Swing, big band, boogie woogie, blues RYM A saxophone honks a two-bar riff, a piano hammers boogie woogie patterns underneath, and a singer half-shouts a lyric about drinking, dancin...

item.title: Skiffle
item.description: Folk → Contemporary folk → Skiffle Period: 1954–1958 Location: United Kingdom (London, Liverpool, Glasgow, Manchester) Key influences: American folk, country blues, jug band music, prison work songs, traditional jazz RYM Lonnie Donegan’s “Rock Island Line” opens with spoken narration about a toll-do...

item.title: British beat boom
item.description: Rock → British beat boom Location: United Kingdom (Liverpool, then London, then the provinces) Period: 1963–1966 Key genres: Beat music, Merseybeat, British rhythm & blues, mod RYM For about three years in the early sixties, the sound of the British singles chart was three guitars, a hammered ba...

item.title: Greenwich Village folk scene
item.description: Folk → Greenwich Village folk scene Location: Greenwich Village, Manhattan, New York City Period: 1958–1965 Key genres: Folk, topical song, folk rock RYM On any given night in 1962, you could walk down MacDougal Street and hear Appalachian murder ballads in one basement, Delta blues in another, and ...

item.title: Merseybeat
item.description: Pop | Rock → Pop rock → Beat → Merseybeat Location: Liverpool, England Period: 1958–1966 Key genres: Beat music, rock & roll, pop rock RYM Merseybeat was the sound of a port that heard America first: rock and roll and rhythm and blues carried off the Atlantic boats, sped up and sung back in a Sc...

item.title: Call and response
item.description: Also known as: antiphony, lead-and-response, the preacher and the congregation First heard in: West African communal music, carried across the Atlantic through work songs, ring shouts, and field hollers; crystallized in African American gospel and spirituals in the nineteenth and early twentieth cen...

item.title: Modal interchange
item.description: Also known as: modal mixture, borrowed chords First heard in: Western classical harmony; became a defining harmonic texture in 1960s pop and rock through the Beatles, Brian Wilson, and Burt Bacharach Related concepts: I–vi–IV–V progression, Pentatonic and blues scales The coda of “Hey Jude” (1968) r...

item.title: Beat
item.description: Pop | Rock → Pop rock → Beat Period: c.

item.title: Rock & roll
item.description: Rock → Rock & roll Period: Mid-1950s–early 1960s Location: Memphis, New Orleans, Chicago, New York Key influences: Rhythm & blues, country, gospel, boogie woogie Playlist: ROLL RYM The sound of convergence.
